title: AI History — Societal Impact and the Engineering Responsibility
key_insight: Every AI paradigm shift has created and destroyed industries, jobs, and research communities. Engineers who understand history make better technical and ethical decisions.
body:
## The Hype-to-Reality Calibration Problem

The AI field has a systematic credibility problem:

```
1956: 'Human-level AI in 20 years'
1980: Expert systems will replace doctors, lawyers, engineers
2016: Self-driving cars will be everywhere by 2021
2022: AGI within 5 years (multiple credible researchers)

Pattern: experts consistently over-estimate short-term capability
         and under-estimate long-term impact
```

The Amara's Law formulation: *'We tend to overestimate the effect of a technology in the short run and underestimate the effect in the long run.'*

## The Compute-Data-Algorithms Triangle

```python
# Three inputs to AI progress — historically decoupled, now interconnected:

# Compute growth (Moore's Law + GPU specialisation):
# 1950: ~1 FLOP/s
# 2012: 10^15 FLOP/s (NVIDIA GTX 580, 1.58 TFLOPS)
# 2024: 10^18 FLOP/s (NVIDIA H100, 3.35 PFLOPS)

# Data growth (the internet):
# Common Crawl 2023: 3.4 billion web pages
# FineWeb (educational): 15 trillion tokens
# The Pile: 825GB of diverse text

# Algorithm improvements (efficiency):
# Transformers reduced training time 10x vs RNNs
# Flash Attention reduced memory 5-20x
# LoRA fine-tuning reduced trainable params 10000x
```

## The Current Inflection: Test-Time Compute

The 2024-2025 shift: instead of just scaling training compute, scaling **inference** compute:

```
OpenAI o1 (2024): allocates more compute at inference time
Used for:         reasoning, math, code, multi-step problems
Mechanism:        chain-of-thought, search, self-critique

This is analogous to:
  Training compute = how hard you studied
  Inference compute = how long you think before answering
```

## Engineering Responsibility: Lessons From History

Expert systems in the 1980s were deployed for medical diagnosis without validation — and caused harm. Image classifiers in 2018 had racial bias baked in from training data. Language models in 2023 generated confident misinformation at scale.

The pattern: **capability races outrun safety work**.

What history-aware engineers do differently:
- Build evaluation before building the model
- Test for failure modes on underrepresented groups
- Monitor post-deployment, not just at release
- Document limitations explicitly in model cards
- Push back on deployment timelines when safety is unresolved
common_mistakes: Building AI products without reading model cards and understanding documented limitations
Assuming 'worked in lab = works in production' — distribution shift from test to real world is the norm, not the exception
Not investing in evals — the biggest failure mode in AI products is discovering you're measuring the wrong thing
mini_exercise: Read the Wikipedia article on 'COMPAS (software)' — the AI system used for criminal risk assessment. What went wrong? What was the technical failure? What was the societal impact? How should it have been evaluated before deployment?
